title: Third-party app stores coming to Google Play next week as Epic settlement withdrawn
author: Ryan Whitwam
gist: Google and Epic withdrew their proposed settlement modification after an MIT economist testified it wouldn't let rivals overcome Google Play's network effects, meaning Judge Donato's original antitrust remedy stands: Google must begin distributing competing third-party app stores directly inside Google Play starting July 22, opening Android's app distribution to real competition.

themes.theme: Court-ordered opening of Android app distribution

insights.claim: Google confirmed it will begin distributing rival third-party app stores directly inside Google Play starting July 22, after Google and Epic withdrew their motion to modify Judge James Donato's original antitrust injunction, meaning the harsher original remedy — not the softer 2025 settlement — will now take effect.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated catalyst (July 22) that ends Google Play's exclusive distribution monopoly on Android in the US; watch for share shifts in mobile app distribution/monetization and reduced effective take-rate on Google Play as competing storefronts (with lower fees) gain default access to the full app catalog.

insights.claim: MIT economics professor Nancy Rose, in expert testimony to the court, found the Google-Epic settlement was 'unlikely to enable Google Play's potential competitors to overcome their long-standing network-effect disadvantage in a timely manner,' undermining the settlement's rationale before its withdrawal.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Expert economic testimony that voluntary/negotiated remedies are insufficient against platform network effects supports stronger structural remedies going forward — a precedent relevant to other ongoing app-store and platform antitrust cases globally.
insights.quote: unlikely to enable Google Play’s potential competitors to overcome their long-standing network-effect disadvantage in a timely manner

insights.claim: Under the reinstated original injunction, third-party app stores distributed via Google Play get default access to Google Play's full app catalog (developers can opt out), and Google can charge a $5,000 annual fee for security/compliance review, with removal from the program if more than 1 percent of a store's app installs are flagged as malware.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Defines the concrete economics and compliance bar for new entrants (e.g., Epic's own store, Samsung Galaxy Store, Amazon Appstore) trying to compete on Android — a low $5,000 fee and catalog mirroring materially lowers the barrier to entry versus Google Play's historical 15-30% commission model.
insights.locator: In a brief filing, Google's legal team

insights.claim: Google's Registered App Store program, the centerpiece of the now-abandoned settlement, will still roll out globally per the original Google-Epic settlement terms, but will likely only be reflected inside the Google Play Store for US users, while the court's original remedy applies more broadly in the US.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Creates a bifurcated global vs. US regulatory landscape for Android app distribution that investors in app-store-adjacent businesses should track separately by region.
insights.locator: The changes Google has announced under the Epic agreement will proceed for now
